positions.proponents: Jesus is lamenting that the Temple system has consumed this widow’s last resources, fulfilling 12:40 (“they devour widows’ houses”). Her giving is tragic, not exemplary.
positions.argument: Indictment of the system

title: Does “this generation” refer to AD 70 or to a future generation?
question: Does “this generation” refer to AD 70 or to a future generation?

positions.label: AD 70 view
positions.proponents: Genea consistently means the contemporary generation in Mark. The signs of vv.5-23 fit the Jewish War of AD 66-70. The prediction was fulfilled within a generation of Jesus’s lifetime.
positions.argument: Near-term fulfilment

positions.label: Future generation view
positions.proponents: “This generation” refers to the generation that sees the specific signs of vv.14-22 begin. The ultimate parousia (vv.24-27) was not fulfilled in AD 70.
positions.argument: Future eschatological generation

title: Was the Last Supper a Passover meal?
question: Was the Last Supper a Passover meal?

positions.label: Yes — Passover meal
positions.proponents: Mark 14:12-17 sets the meal on Nisan 14/15, when the Passover lamb was sacrificed. The Passover framework is explicit in the preparation narrative.
positions.argument: Passover meal

positions.label: No — the day before
positions.proponents: John 18:28 places the crucifixion on the Day of Preparation, meaning the Last Supper was the evening before Passover. Jesus dies as the lambs are being slaughtered in the Temple.
positions.argument: Pre-Passover meal

title: What did the torn veil signify?
question: What did the torn veil signify?

positions.label: Access to God
positions.argument: Access to the Most Holy Place

positions.label: Temple judgment
positions.proponents: The tearing is a sign of God’s judgment on the Temple system, anticipating its physical destruction in AD 70.
positions.argument: Judgment on the Temple

title: Where does the original Mark end?
question: Where does the original Mark end?

positions.label: 16:8 original ending
positions.argument: Original ending at 16:8

positions.label: 16:9-20 canonical ending
positions.proponents: The longer ending is included in the NT canon (Irenaeus quotes it as early as c.AD 185) and has been the church’s received text for most of Christian history.
positions.argument: Canonical longer ending

title: Is Mary's consent to the Annunciation essential to the Incarnation?
question: Is Mary's consent to the Annunciation essential to the Incarnation?

positions.label: Necessary consent view
positions.proponents: Mary’s "may your word to me be fulfilled" (1:38) is a genuine consent without which the Incarnation would not proceed. The Incarnation required a human yes.
positions.argument: Consent as condition

positions.label: Divine sovereignty view
positions.proponents: God’s election of Mary was unconditional; her consent is the gracious response to what God was already doing, not a condition of its occurrence.
positions.argument: Election precedes consent

title: Was the census historical?
question: Was the census historical?

positions.label: Historical view
positions.proponents: Luke’s census fits within the Augustan census programme (documented by Josephus and papyri). An earlier Quirinius census or a different official is possible.
positions.argument: Historical

positions.label: Anachronistic view
positions.proponents: Luke may have confused a later AD 6 Quirinius census with Herod’s reign. The historical difficulty is acknowledged even among conservative scholars.
positions.argument: Possible error in attribution

title: Does Luke’s genealogy represent Mary’s or Joseph’s lineage?
question: Does Luke’s genealogy represent Mary’s or Joseph’s lineage?

positions.label: Joseph’s legal lineage
positions.proponents: Luke traces Joseph’s legal ancestry through Nathan; Matthew traces his biological ancestry through Solomon. Both are true of Joseph simultaneously.
positions.argument: Both genealogies are Joseph’s

positions.label: Mary’s biological lineage
positions.proponents: Some propose that Luke’s “Heli” was Mary’s father, making this her lineage transmitted through Joseph’s legal adoption.
positions.argument: Mary’s lineage

title: Did the Nazareth rejection precede or follow early Galilean ministry?
question: Did the Nazareth rejection precede or follow early Galilean ministry?

positions.label: Luke’s order original
positions.proponents: Luke is following a topical arrangement, placing the Nazareth scene first as a programmatic statement of the mission.
positions.argument: Programmatic placement

positions.label: Mark/Matthew order original
positions.proponents: Mark 6 and Matthew 13 place the Nazareth rejection after substantial Galilean ministry. Luke has moved it forward for theological reasons.
positions.argument: Topical rearrangement

title: Is the miraculous catch in Luke 5 the same event as John 21?
question: Is the miraculous catch in Luke 5 the same event as John 21?

positions.label: Two different events
positions.proponents: Luke 5 occurs at the beginning of Jesus’s ministry; John 21 occurs after the resurrection. Though structurally parallel, they are different events with different theological functions.
positions.argument: Two events

positions.label: One event relocated
positions.proponents: Some argue John 21 was originally a pre-resurrection calling story that has been relocated. The similarities are too close to be coincidental.
positions.argument: Possible relocation

title: Are Luke’s beatitudes and Matthew’s from the same sermon?
question: Are Luke’s beatitudes and Matthew’s from the same sermon?

positions.label: One sermon, two accounts
positions.proponents: Matthew and Luke preserve different aspects of the same teaching event. Matthew has expanded and arranged thematically; Luke has the more original form.
positions.argument: Same sermon

positions.label: Two different sermons
positions.proponents: Jesus taught similar material on multiple occasions. Luke and Matthew reflect different teaching events.
positions.argument: Two occasions

title: Is Luke 7's anointing the same as John 12?
question: Is Luke 7's anointing the same as John 12?

positions.label: Different events
positions.proponents: Luke's anointing is earlier, by a sinful woman; John 12's is by Mary of Bethany before the Passion. The theological purposes are different.
positions.argument: Two events

positions.label: Same event, relocated
positions.proponents: The structural parallels are close enough that some argue Luke has relocated the Bethany anointing.
positions.argument: One relocated event

title: Did the women travel with Jesus full-time or provide financial support from their homes?
question: Did the women travel with Jesus full-time or provide financial support from their homes?

positions.label: Full-time travel
positions.proponents: 8:1-3 implies they traveled with the Twelve — "from one town and village to another... The Twelve were with him, and also some women."
positions.argument: Itinerant

positions.label: Financial supporters from home
positions.proponents: The women provided resources from their own means while primarily based at home, not necessarily on the full traveling circuit.
positions.argument: Home-based

title: What does 9:27 mean: "some standing here will not taste death before they see the kingdom of God"?
question: What does 9:27 mean: "some standing here will not taste death before they see the kingdom of God"?

positions.label: The Transfiguration
positions.proponents: The most natural referent: the Transfiguration follows immediately (9:28) and is a proleptic vision of the kingdom’s glory.
positions.argument: Transfiguration

positions.label: Pentecost and the church
positions.proponents: The kingdom’s visible arrival in the community of the Spirit, experienced by those who survived to Pentecost.
positions.argument: Pentecost

title: Is the Good Samaritan an allegory?
question: Is the Good Samaritan an allegory?

positions.label: Pure parable
positions.proponents: The parable answers the question of who is a neighbor. The allegorical interpretation (Adam = mankind, Samaritan = Christ, etc.) is a secondary patristic imposition on the text.
positions.argument: Historical-literal

positions.label: Christological allegory
positions.proponents: Origen’s reading has the Samaritan as Christ is consistent with Luke’s Christology and the language of compassion used for Jesus throughout.
positions.argument: Christological

title: The "Sign of Jonah": Preaching or Resurrection?
question: The "Sign of Jonah": Preaching or Resurrection?

positions.label: Luke's emphasis: the preacher
positions.argument: Luke's version focuses on Jonah as a preacher — his whole mission to Nineveh is the sign. The Son of Man's preached word will be the sign to this generation.

positions.label: Matthew's emphasis: the resurrection
positions.argument: Matthew (12:40) specifies the three days in the fish as a type of the resurrection. The sign is death and vindication.

title: The Lord's Prayer: One Form or Two?
question: The Lord's Prayer: One Form or Two?

positions.label: Luke preserves the original
positions.argument: Luke's shorter form is likely closer to the original. Matthew expanded it for liturgical use — the additions are consistent with his other expansions of Q material.

positions.label: Matthew preserves the original
positions.argument: Matthew's fuller form may reflect a carefully transmitted liturgical tradition from the Jerusalem community.

title: Does 12:49 refer to the fire of the Spirit or the fire of judgment?
question: Does 12:49 refer to the fire of the Spirit or the fire of judgment?

positions.label: Fire of the Spirit
positions.argument: The fire Jesus came to cast (v.49) is the fire of the Holy Spirit poured out at Pentecost (Acts 2:3-4). The "baptism" of v.50 and the Spirit's fire are two sides of the same event.

positions.label: Fire of judgment and division
positions.argument: The fire is the eschatological judgment that Jesus's arrival initiates — consistent with John the Baptist's fire-and-Spirit imagery (3:16-17) and the division language of vv.51-53.

title: Is 12:47-48 teaching degrees of punishment in hell?
question: Is 12:47-48 teaching degrees of punishment in hell?

positions.label: Yes, graduated eschatological punishment
positions.argument: The passage implies that punishment in the final judgment is proportionate to knowledge and responsibility — a teaching consistent with Rom 2:12-16 and Heb 10:29.

positions.label: Contextual hyperbole about earthly discipline
positions.argument: The punishment language refers to the consequences of unfaithfulness in this life and in the community, not necessarily to eternal states.

title: Does 13:35 refer to the Palm Sunday entry or to the Parousia?
question: Does 13:35 refer to the Palm Sunday entry or to the Parousia?

positions.label: Palm Sunday
positions.argument: The immediate narrative context supports a near-term reference: 19:38 records the crowd's "Blessed is the king who comes in the name of the Lord!" as Jesus enters Jerusalem. The saying is fulfilled within the same travel narrative.

positions.label: The Parousia
positions.argument: The full recognition implied — Jerusalem "seeing" Jesus with the Hallel acclamation — was not achieved at the Palm Sunday entry, where Jesus was rejected within days. The ultimate fulfilment is eschatological.

title: The Great Banquet: Israel and the Gentiles?
question: The Great Banquet: Israel and the Gentiles?

positions.label: Three-stage allegorical reading
positions.argument: The three invitation stages correspond to: (1) the religious establishment's refusal; (2) inclusion of tax collectors and sinners within Israel; (3) the Gentile mission. The parable encodes the history of the early church's mission.

positions.label: Social criticism without allegory
positions.argument: The parable is primarily a subversive social critique of elite reciprocity culture, not an allegory of salvation history. The allegorical reading domesticates what is a more radical social challenge.

title: Is the Elder Son Condemned or Invited?
question: Is the Elder Son Condemned or Invited?

positions.label: Elder son condemned
positions.argument: The elder's resentment represents a fundamental failure of love that the parable exposes as disqualifying. He is the Pharisees; his exclusion from the feast is his own choice and its consequences are permanent.

positions.label: Elder son invited, not condemned
positions.argument: The parable does not end with the elder son refusing. The father goes out and pleads — as he went out to the younger. The open ending is an invitation: the Pharisees can still enter. The parable is evangelistic toward the elder son, not merely critical.

title: Is the Parable Literal Theology or Narrative Illustration?
question: Is the Parable Literal Theology or Narrative Illustration?

positions.label: Literal afterlife geography
positions.argument: The parable describes actual conditions in the intermediate state: conscious existence, recognition, memory, the chasm, and the finality of post-death conditions. Jesus is teaching doctrine through narrative.

positions.label: Narrative illustration using conventional imagery
positions.argument: Jesus uses the conventional Jewish imagery of Hades (from 1 Enoch, rabbinic tradition) to illustrate his point about wealth and poverty. The point is the reversal and the sufficiency of Scripture, not a map of the afterlife.

title: "The kingdom of God is in your midst" or "within you"?
question: "The kingdom of God is in your midst" or "within you"?

positions.label: Within you (interior)
positions.argument: Entos normally means "within/inside." The kingdom is an interior spiritual reality present in the hearts of those who receive it. This reading shaped centuries of Christian spirituality.

positions.label: In your midst (among you)
positions.proponents: Most modern scholars: Fitzmyer, Green, Marshall, Bock
positions.argument: Jesus would not say the kingdom is "within" the Pharisees who oppose him. Luke consistently presents the kingdom as present in Jesus's ministry and person. Entos can mean "among" (Ps 109:30 LXX uses it this way).

title: Why does Jesus ask "Why do you call me good?" (18:19)?
question: Why does Jesus ask "Why do you call me good?" (18:19)?

positions.label: Jesus denies his own goodness
positions.argument: Jesus is deflecting the title "good teacher" because he does not consider himself divine. This reading uses the saying to argue against early high Christology.

positions.label: Jesus is testing the ruler's theology
positions.argument: Jesus is not denying his own goodness but pressing the ruler: do you understand what you're saying? "Good" belongs to God alone — so who do you think I am? The question is an invitation to Christological recognition, not a denial of divinity.

positions.label: Jesus is correcting flattery
positions.argument: The ruler is using "Good teacher" as a social honorific. Jesus redirects: goodness is a divine attribute, not a teaching compliment. The saying is about theological seriousness, not Christology.

title: Is Zacchaeus's statement in 19:8 a promise or a defence?
question: Is Zacchaeus's statement in 19:8 a promise or a defence?

positions.label: A promise: present conversion
positions.argument: Zacchaeus's present tense ("I give" / "I will pay back") represents a declaration of immediate intent — his spontaneous response to Jesus's presence. This reading makes the story a conversion narrative.

positions.label: A defence: describing current practice
positions.argument: Zacchaeus is defending himself against the crowd's accusation: "Look, I already do give half... and when I have defrauded someone, I already do repay fourfold." On this reading, the story is about unjust accusation, not conversion.

title: What is the "image of God" implication in 20:24-25?
question: What is the "image of God" implication in 20:24-25?

positions.label: Coin/tax only
positions.argument: Jesus is making a practical point about Roman taxation: coins bearing Caesar's image belong in Caesar's domain; taxes are therefore legitimate. The God-claim is about Temple tithes and religious obligations.

positions.label: Humans as God's image
positions.argument: Since humans bear God's image (Gen 1:26), the full answer to "give to God what is God's" is: give yourself — totally — to God. The eikōn wordplay is intentional and subversive: Caesar gets his coins; God gets everything that bears his image, which is you.

title: "This generation" in 21:32: Near or Far Fulfilment?
question: "This generation" in 21:32: Near or Far Fulfilment?

positions.label: Entirely fulfilled in AD 70
positions.proponents: Preterist interpreters: N.T. Wright, R.C. Sproul
positions.argument: "This generation" refers to the generation alive in AD 30; "all these things" refers to the Jerusalem destruction of AD 70. Luke 21:20-24 describes the siege; the Parousia language (21:25-27) is cosmic imagery for a historical event.

positions.label: Partially fulfilled in AD 70, fully at Parousia
positions.argument: The near events (AD 70) and far events (Parousia) are both in view; "this generation" may extend beyond a single human lifetime. A double fulfilment, with AD 70 as a type of the final end.

positions.label: "Generation" = the Jewish people
positions.argument: "This generation" (genea) means the Jewish people as an enduring race — they will persist until the final eschatological fulfilment.

title: Is Luke 22:43-44 (the angel and the bloody sweat) original?
question: Is Luke 22:43-44 (the angel and the bloody sweat) original?

positions.label: Original
positions.argument: The passage is present in many important early witnesses and absent mainly in Alexandrian manuscripts. Its omission may reflect early theological embarrassment about Christ's agony and its implications for his divinity. The stylistic features are consistent with Luke.

positions.label: Later addition
positions.argument: The best Alexandrian witnesses (p75, the corrector of Sinaiticus) omit it. Early Church Fathers sometimes seem unaware of it. The theological difficulty it presents may explain its original absence rather than subsequent omission.

title: Is "Father, forgive them" (23:34a) original?
question: Is "Father, forgive them" (23:34a) original?

positions.label: Original
positions.argument: Present in Sinaiticus, A, C, D and many others. Absent in p75, B, W and a few others. Internal evidence strongly favours inclusion: stylistic consistency with Luke, the prayer for persecutors echoed in Acts 7:60 (Stephen), and the obvious motive for early omission (anti-Jewish sentiment).

positions.label: Later addition
positions.proponents: Some scholars, Westcott-Hort originally excluded it
positions.argument: The best Alexandrian witnesses (p75, B) lack it. The motive for addition is clear (theological enhancement). The silence of some early Fathers may indicate non-awareness.

title: Does the Emmaus recognition at the breaking of bread presuppose a eucharistic setting?
question: Does the Emmaus recognition at the breaking of bread presuppose a eucharistic setting?

positions.label: Yes, eucharistic allusion is deliberate
positions.proponents: Fitzmyer, Green, most Catholic and many Protestant scholars
positions.argument: The sequence — took bread, gave thanks, broke it, gave it (24:30) — exactly parallels the Last Supper (22:19) and the feeding of the five thousand (9:16). Luke uses this four-verb pattern consistently for the Eucharist. The recognition at the breaking of bread is a model for Eucharistic presence of the risen Christ.

positions.label: Ordinary hospitality, not eucharistic
positions.argument: The disciples are guests at Emmaus, not officiants at a sacrament. The four-verb pattern is simply how meals are described. The theological point is about hospitality as the occasion of recognition, not about a specific sacramental theology.

title: Is the Prologue a Pre-existing Hymn?
question: Is the Prologue a Pre-existing Hymn?

positions.label: Yes: hymnic adaptation
positions.argument: The prologue has a poetic, rhythmic structure distinct from the surrounding narrative, with prose insertions about John the Baptist (vv.6-8, 15). The original hymn may have been a Johannine community hymn to the Logos that the evangelist adapted.

positions.label: No: unified composition
positions.argument: The supposed hymnic structure is not consistent enough to identify a discrete pre-existing hymn. The prologue is the evangelist's own introduction, written in elevated prose that reflects the content's grandeur.

title: One Temple Cleansing or Two?
question: One Temple Cleansing or Two?

positions.label: Two cleansings: one at start, one at end
positions.argument: The differences between John and the Synoptics (timing, details, context) are best explained by two separate events. Jesus's prophetic action would naturally provoke escalating hostility over three years.

positions.label: One cleansing: John moved it for theological reasons
positions.argument: John deliberately placed the cleansing at the beginning to introduce the temple-body theme early. Historical-critical scholarship generally prefers the Synoptic timing as more historically plausible and sees John's placement as theological redaction.

title: "Water and Spirit" in 3:5: Baptism or Regeneration?
question: "Water and Spirit" in 3:5: Baptism or Regeneration?

positions.label: Water = baptism
positions.proponents: Catholic and many patristic interpreters, Luther
positions.argument: The natural reading of "water" in a baptism context (John 3 follows John's baptising ministry) is Christian baptism. The new birth is sacramentally enacted in baptism. Augustine, Chrysostom, and Luther all hold this view.

positions.label: Water = the Spirit's cleansing work (hendiadys)
positions.argument: Water and Spirit describe the same event — the Spirit's purifying regenerative work promised in Ezek 36:25-27. The OT background and the immediate context (Nicodemus doesn't know baptism yet) favour this reading.

positions.label: Water = physical birth
positions.argument: "Born of water" = amniotic fluid (natural birth); "born of Spirit" = spiritual birth. Jesus is distinguishing natural from spiritual birth.

title: Is the Samaritan Woman a Historical Figure or a Symbol?
question: Is the Samaritan Woman a Historical Figure or a Symbol?

positions.label: Historical and symbolic
positions.argument: The encounter is historical (specific geographical details, personal details about the woman's life) but also carries symbolic resonance (five husbands = five foreign nations of 2 Kgs 17; the woman = Samaria as bride). Both levels operate simultaneously.

positions.label: Primarily symbolic
positions.argument: The woman represents Samaria seeking its true husband (God); the five husbands are the five gods imported by the five nations. The personal details are allegorical rather than biographical.

title: Realised vs. Future Eschatology in John 5
question: Realised vs. Future Eschatology in John 5

positions.label: Primarily realised
positions.argument: The emphasis in John is on present possession: "has eternal life," "has crossed over from death to life" (v.24). Future resurrection language (vv.28-29) may be a later ecclesiastical addition to John's original text.

positions.label: Both present and future, complementary
positions.argument: Both dimensions are authentically Johannine and authentically dominical. Present realisation of eternal life (v.24-25) and future bodily resurrection (v.28-29) are both affirmed; they describe different aspects of the same salvation. The Gospel maintains this both/and throughout.

title: Is John 6:51-58 Eucharistic?
question: Is John 6:51-58 Eucharistic?

positions.label: Yes: reference to the Eucharist
positions.proponents: Catholic, Orthodox, many Protestant scholars (Brown, Hoskyns)
positions.argument: The language shifts to the physical trōgō; the flesh/blood pairing is the eucharistic formula; the synagogue setting doesn't preclude post-resurrection sacramental reference. John deliberately replaces the Last Supper institution with this discourse.

positions.label: No: metaphorical reference to faith
positions.argument: Verse 63 ("the flesh counts for nothing; the Spirit gives life") interprets the eating as spiritual. The discourse is about faith (v.29, 35, 40, 47) throughout; the "flesh and blood" intensification is a figure for complete appropriation of Christ.

positions.label: Both: the discourse is about faith, sacramentally enacted in the Eucharist
positions.argument: The discourse addresses faith in Christ; the Eucharist is the visible enactment of that faith. The sacrament is the appointed means of the spiritual eating the discourse describes.

title: Where do the "rivers of living water" flow from — the believer or Jesus?
question: Where do the "rivers of living water" flow from — the believer or Jesus?

positions.label: From the believer
positions.argument: "Whoever believes in me, as Scripture has said, rivers of living water will flow from within them." The believer becomes a source of life for others through the indwelling Spirit.

positions.label: From Jesus
positions.proponents: Some scholars (Origen, some modern interpreters), supported by punctuation
positions.argument: Punctuate vv.37-38 as: "Let anyone who is thirsty come to me, and let the one who believes in me drink. As Scripture has said, rivers of living water will flow from within him [Jesus]." The "him" refers to Jesus; the Spirit flows from the glorified Christ.

title: Does "before Abraham was, I am" assert eternal deity or simply pre-existence?
question: Does "before Abraham was, I am" assert eternal deity or simply pre-existence?

positions.label: Full divine name claim — eternal deity
positions.proponents: Most evangelical scholars, MacArthur, Carson, Keener
positions.argument: The egō eimi without predicate, the deliberate grammatical contrast (aorist genesthai vs present eimi), and the immediate stoning attempt (for blasphemy) all confirm a divine name claim. This is not merely pre-existence but eternal self-existent being.

positions.label: Pre-existence, not necessarily full deity
positions.argument: The statement asserts Jesus existed before Abraham; the "I am" is the natural Greek form for "I exist." The stoning may be for the whole discourse, not this specific phrase. This reading requires underweighting both the grammar and the opponents' response.

title: What is the purpose of the man's congenital blindness?
question: What is the purpose of the man's congenital blindness?

positions.label: Divine purpose: revelation
positions.argument: God ordained the blindness for this purpose: that the works of God might be displayed. This does not make God the author of evil but means that God in his sovereignty uses suffering for redemptive ends.

positions.label: Natural misfortune repurposed
positions.argument: Jesus is not saying God caused the blindness but that God can use the blindness. The "so that" is consequential rather than purposive: given the blindness, the works of God will be displayed. This reading softens the providential claim.

positions.label: Eschatological purpose only
positions.argument: The man represents "the world" generically; the healing represents the eschatological restoration. The narrative is primarily symbolic rather than historical.

title: "I and the Father are one" — unity of essence or unity of purpose?
question: "I and the Father are one" — unity of essence or unity of purpose?

positions.label: Unity of essence/nature (orthodox Trinitarianism)
positions.proponents: Athanasius, Calvin, MacArthur, most orthodox interpreters
positions.argument: The neuter hen asserts a unity of divine substance. The opponents' immediate response (stoning for blasphemy, "you claim to be God," v.33) confirms a divine nature claim. Jesus does not deny the charge; he defends the claim's consistency with Scripture.

positions.label: Unity of purpose/will only
positions.proponents: Arianism, Jehovah's Witnesses, some liberal scholars
positions.argument: Hen refers to unity of purpose and mission, not ontological identity. The Father and Son work together as one. This reading cannot account for the stoning response or for John's prologue ("the Word was God").

positions.label: Both — inseparable
positions.argument: The unity of purpose (everything the Son does the Father does, ch.5) and the unity of essence are not separable in John's theology. The hen statement encompasses both, grounded in the ontological.

title: Why did Jesus weep if he knew he was about to raise Lazarus?
question: Why did Jesus weep if he knew he was about to raise Lazarus?

positions.label: Genuine solidarity with human grief
positions.argument: Jesus wept because death is genuinely grievous. His foreknowledge of the resurrection does not negate the real grief of the moment; it contextualises it. He weeps for Mary and Martha and for the condition of humanity under death, even as he is about to overcome it.

positions.label: Anger at death's power
positions.proponents: Some scholars reading embrimaomai as indignation
positions.argument: The groaning in v.33 (embrimaomai) is an expression of righteous anger at death and the evil that produces it. The tears are the overflow of that anger. Jesus weeps not as a victim of grief but as the conqueror of death who is angry at its domain.

positions.label: Both
positions.argument: The weeping is both genuine grief (solidarity with the sisters) and something harder to name — perhaps the weight of what is being asked of him, the shadow of his own coming death over the scene.

title: Does "I will draw all people" teach universal salvation?
question: Does "I will draw all people" teach universal salvation?

positions.label: No: "all" means all kinds / all the elect
positions.argument: "All" (pantas) in John often means "all without distinction" rather than "all without exception" (cf. 1:7; 3:26; 4:25; 11:48). The context (Gentiles as well as Jews) supports "all peoples/all nations." The drawing is effective for those drawn.

positions.label: Yes: universal atonement scope, not universal salvation
positions.argument: "All people" means every individual — the cross provides sufficient atonement for all. Whether all are saved depends on their response. The drawing is real but resistible (6:44 shows that drawing is necessary; it does not show it is irresistible).

positions.label: Universal salvation (apokatastasis)
positions.argument: The universal drawing combined with the universal love (3:16) and Paul's "all in all" (1 Cor 15:28) teaches the eventual salvation of all. Most mainstream scholars regard this as reading too much into any single passage.

title: Is the foot-washing in John 13 a sacrament?
question: Is the foot-washing in John 13 a sacrament?

positions.label: Yes: an ordinance / third sacrament
positions.proponents: Some Free Church traditions, Anabaptists, certain Pentecostals
positions.argument: "Unless I wash you, you have no part with me" (v.8) gives the washing a necessity that parallels baptism. Some traditions practice foot-washing as a required ordinance alongside baptism and the Lord's Supper.

positions.label: No: an example only
positions.argument: The command in v.14-15 is exemplary, not sacramental. The washing illustrates the principle of servant-love; it does not constitute a sacrament. John does not present it with the covenantal framework given to baptism or the Lord's Supper.

positions.label: Both exemplary and spiritually significant
positions.argument: The washing carries both moral (service) and spiritual (cleansing) dimensions without being a full sacrament in the technical sense. Its washing language (v.8: "unless I wash you") links it to baptism without identifying it with baptism.

title: "The Father is greater than I" — economic or ontological subordination?
question: "The Father is greater than I" — economic or ontological subordination?

positions.label: Economic subordination (incarnation only)
positions.argument: The Father's greatness refers to the Son's voluntary submission in the Incarnation. In the eternal relations, the Son is equal to the Father (1:1; 10:30); in the incarnate mission, the Son is functionally subordinate. The statement belongs to the economy, not the ontology.

positions.label: Eternal ontological subordination
positions.argument: The Father is ontologically greater — the Son is the first creation or a lesser divine being. The statement reflects the eternal order of being.

positions.label: Relational/personal subordination within equal essence
positions.argument: The eternal relations within the Trinity include a permanent relational order (Father-Son-Spirit) that involves authority/submission without ontological inequality. The Son eternally submits to the Father's authority while sharing the same divine nature.

title: What happens to branches that are "cut off" in 15:2, 6?
question: What happens to branches that are "cut off" in 15:2, 6?

positions.label: Genuine believers who fall away (arminian)
positions.argument: The branches are genuinely "in" Christ — truly saved — but can be cut off through persistent unfruitfulness or apostasy. The warning is genuine and addressed to real believers.

positions.label: False professors who were never truly saved (calvinist)
positions.argument: The fruitless branches had an external connection to Jesus (in the covenant community, in the church) but were never genuinely regenerate. Their removal reveals that they were never truly his (cf. 1 John 2:19).

positions.label: Loss of reward, not salvation (some dispensationalists)
positions.argument: The branches represent the believer's works, not the believer's person. Unfruitful branches lose rewards at the judgment but not salvation.

title: What does "I have overcome the world" mean?
question: What does "I have overcome the world" mean?

positions.label: Completed victory: the cross as the decisive defeat of evil
positions.proponents: Aulen (Christus Victor), most evangelical and orthodox scholars
positions.argument: The perfect tense declares a completed victory. The cross and resurrection constitute the decisive defeat of the world-system, Satan, sin, and death. The victory is ontologically complete; its full manifestation awaits the parousia.

positions.label: Moral victory: Jesus's faithfulness under pressure
positions.argument: "Overcoming the world" means maintaining integrity and love despite the world's opposition — a moral example rather than a cosmic event. This reading underweights both the perfect tense and the cosmic scope of "world" (kosmos) in John.

positions.label: Eschatological victory: not yet completed
positions.argument: The victory is proleptic — declared in anticipation of the final eschatological completion. It is certain but not yet fully realised.

title: What kind of unity does Jesus pray for in 17:21?
question: What kind of unity does Jesus pray for in 17:21?

positions.label: Spiritual/invisible unity of genuine believers
positions.argument: The unity is the organic unity of those who share the same divine life in Christ. It is invisible in the sense that only God can see it fully, but it produces visible fruits (love, shared mission, mutual service).

positions.label: Visible institutional unity of all Christian denominations
positions.proponents: Catholic, Orthodox, and ecumenical Protestant scholars
positions.argument: The purpose clause ("that the world may believe") requires visible unity. Invisible spiritual unity that leaves the church fragmented into competing denominations does not serve the missiological purpose of the prayer.

positions.label: Both, held in tension
positions.argument: Genuine spiritual unity (sharing the same life in Christ) must be expressed in visible ways (cooperation, mutual recognition, shared witness) to fulfil the prayer's missiological purpose. Neither invisible unity alone nor institutional merger alone satisfies the prayer.

title: Does "my kingdom is not of this world" teach political quietism?
question: Does "my kingdom is not of this world" teach political quietism?

positions.label: Yes: the church should not engage in political power
positions.argument: "Not of this world" means Jesus's kingdom operates by entirely different means than earthly kingdoms. The church that seeks political power has misunderstood its king.

positions.label: No: the kingdom engages the world by different means
positions.proponents: Most mainstream Catholic, Reformed, and evangelical scholars
positions.argument: "Not of this world" refers to origin and method, not location or relevance. The kingdom is present in and working through the world — through truth-bearing, justice-seeking, mercy-giving — but by the Spirit's power rather than coercion. The church engages political life by Christ's means: truth, love, and service.

positions.label: The kingdom will eventually transform political structures
positions.proponents: Post-millennial, Kuyperian Reformed, Catholic social teaching
positions.argument: The kingdom's "not-of-this-world" character does not prevent it from reshaping earthly institutions over time through the Spirit's work in history.

title: What do "blood and water" symbolise in 19:34?
question: What do "blood and water" symbolise in 19:34?

positions.label: Medical: physical confirmation of death
positions.proponents: Most medical scholars, many NT commentators
positions.argument: The blood and water are the physiological result of the spear-thrust — a pericardial effusion. The detail confirms the physical reality of the death against any swoon theory. The eyewitness emphasis (v.35) suggests John knew this detail would be challenged.

positions.label: Sacramental: baptism and Eucharist
positions.proponents: Augustine, most Catholic and Orthodox interpreters
positions.argument: Water = baptism; blood = Eucharist. The two sacraments flow from Christ's side as Eve came from Adam's side — the church born from the cross. Chrysostom: "From the side of Christ was born the church, as Eve from the side of the sleeping Adam."

positions.label: Johannine symbolic: Spirit and atonement
positions.argument: In John's own symbolism: water = the Spirit (7:38-39, given after glorification); blood = the atoning death (1:29; 6:53-56). The blood and water testify together to the completed atonement and the forthcoming gift of the Spirit.

title: Is John 20:22 the same event as Pentecost in Acts 2?
question: Is John 20:22 the same event as Pentecost in Acts 2?

positions.label: Different events: proleptic gift then full gift
positions.argument: John 20:22 is a preliminary or anticipatory gift of the Spirit — preparing the disciples for the mission Jesus assigns. Pentecost (Acts 2) is the full empowerment for the worldwide mission. The same Spirit is given in two stages or dimensions.

positions.label: Same event: two descriptions
positions.proponents: Some scholars (Barrett, Carson argues this is possible)
positions.argument: John and Luke describe the same Easter-Pentecost event. John telescopes Pentecost into Easter evening; Luke separates them. The theological meaning is the same.

positions.label: Symbolic gift: John 20:22 is a performed parable
positions.argument: The "breathing" is a symbolic anticipation of the Spirit's coming, not an actual impartation. The disciples don't receive the Spirit until Acts 2.

title: Is John 21 original to the Gospel or a later addition?
question: Is John 21 original to the Gospel or a later addition?

positions.label: A later addition by the same author or a disciple
positions.argument: Chapter 20:30-31 reads as a conclusion; chapter 21 is an appendix. The different location (Galilee vs Jerusalem) and the note about the beloved disciple's death rumour (v.23) suggest it was added after the author's death, possibly by the Johannine community. The style is consistent with the rest of the Gospel.

positions.label: Original, integral part of the Gospel
positions.argument: No manuscript of John lacks ch.21; it was always part of the Gospel. Chapter 20:30-31 is not a "conclusion" but a purpose statement that can appear at the end of a major section (as Paul puts purpose statements mid-letter). Chapter 21 provides the necessary rehabilitation of Peter and the identification of the author.

positions.label: Added by the same author as an epilogue
positions.argument: The style is authentically Johannine; the content (Peter's restoration, author identification) is necessary for the Gospel's completeness. The author added it after the main body was complete, perhaps after the death of the beloved disciple prompted the clarification of v.23.

title: Authorship and Historicity of Scripture Walkthrough
question: Authorship and Historicity of Scripture Walkthrough

positions.label: Traditional Lukan authorship (Hengel, Keener, classical scholarship)
positions.proponents: Attributes Acts to Luke the physician (Col 4:14), a companion of Paul, on the basis of the "we" passages, historical accuracy, and early church tradition.
positions.argument: Luke’s medical vocabulary (noted by Hobart) and the detailed geographical and political accuracy of Acts support the traditional attribution.

positions.label: Anonymous/secondary authorship (Haenchen, Conzelmann)
positions.proponents: Questions Lukan authorship on the basis of discrepancies between Acts’ Paul and the Pauline letters, suggesting a later author using Lukan sources.
positions.argument: This view notes that Acts does not always match Paul’s letters precisely, and argues the "we" passages may be a literary device rather than eyewitness markers.
